name: Teddy Wilson
abstract: Theodore Shaw Wilson (November 24, 1912 – July 31, 1986) was an American jazz pianist. Described by critic Scott Yanow as "the definitive swing pianist", Wilson's piano style was gentle, elegant, and virtuosic.

quotes.text: And then the next important part in the movie, I guess, they have us doing -- oh, after the band -- that's because the band leader in certain scenes where we're playing at the Palomar Ballroom in Los Angeles, I guess, and the success of the band, and all of a sudden, after flopping along the line, then the tremendous success. And then, of course, the movie ends with the Carnegie Hall Concert in 1938, with the tremendous triumph of the band, the first all jazz concert in Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Recalling the historic 1938 Benny Goodman concert

quotes.text: But for the record, I think it should be made clear that that wasn't the first jazz in Carnegie Hall. Count Basie played in Carnegie Hall before Goodman, in a program called "Spiritual Swing." They had the Golden Gate Quartet on there and I think the boogie-woogie pianist, Pete Johnson, Albert Hammond, maybe Leadbelly was on there. And Basie represented the swing. Do you remember that concert?
quotes.caption: Recalling Count Basie's 1938 Spiritual Swing concert

quotes.text: But still, you can't say that the majority is the only criteria, because the mass of people don't go to Carnegie Hall and make Carnegie Hall no good;
quotes.caption: General comment on Carnegie Hall's prestige

quotes.text: But there is another concert that took place before both of those, which I can't recall the name of. But I think, Milt, you told me about it. Something maybe as early as 1928. I don't know if it was in Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Uncertain memory of a potential 1928 Carnegie Hall concert

quotes.text: But I think I can get enough of that, mixed in with the four-a-night-in-the-room, in a 6-night-a-week or a 5-night-a-week job, to make it, keep it interesting. But if I had my druthers, as I said, it would be strictly that concert hall, Carnegie Hall, Symphony Hall, every night -- and no cellars, smoke-filled cellars with prostitutes sitting around, like the Europeans like to take their jazz in the cellar one flight below the street -- none of that!
quotes.caption: Expressing preference for concert halls over smoky clubs

quotes.text: Well, I suppose I could borrow some remarks that have impressed me over the years, like the one about the guy who asks which is the quickest way to Carnegie Hall? Practice! That one is solid, that's a good thing to know.
quotes.caption: Quoting a joke about practicing for Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
about_quotes.evidence: So he used to play the Mozart concerto for flute on saxophone and the Webern concerto for clarinet. But then he played the Benny Goodman version of the Mozart concerto for clarinet, back to back with Benny Goodman at Carnegie Hall with Ziggy Eman, Gene Krupa and Teddy Wilson and Lionel Hampton.
about_quotes.doc_title: Paquito D'Rivera — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: Outside of music, Benny Goodman is a social hero. Because his love for the music was so pure that he just did not understand why he couldn’t have Lionel Hampton in his band, and then Charlie Christian and then Teddy Wilson you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jason Palmer
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee only acknowledges the interviewer's statement about Teddy Wilson and Lionel Hampton with 'Yeah. Un hum. Yup.' without adding personal interaction or relationship details.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jason Palmer interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 2, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Frishberg
about_quotes.evidence: Teddy Wilson still thrills me, just the exuberance of his playing when he was playing in the 1930s on the Billie Holiday records and some of the Benny Goodman small band sextet records.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Frishberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 16, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Buckingham,
about_quotes.evidence: He worked at two labels... I got to know Mr. Hammond in the 80s... He was the guy who integrated the Goodman band in 1935 by getting him to hire Teddy Wilson
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Buckingham, interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, February 25, 2019 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Akiyoshi Toshiko
about_quotes.evidence: he played Teddy Wilson’s “Sweet Lorraine”—this became a very famous story. But when I heard him playing it, I said, “I want to play just like that!”
about_quotes.doc_title: Akiyoshi Toshiko — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
